summary: Italian Renaissance painter and Dominican friar, beatified in 1982. Best known for his Annunciation fresco (Gabriel with multicolored wings approaching Mary in a portico with Corinthian columns) at the San Marco monastery in Florence, and the San Marco Altarpiece. Also painted frescoes in each cell of the convent and decorated the Niccoline Chapel at the Vatican. Patron was Cosimo de' Medici. Luca Signorelli completed his fresco cycle in the San Brizio Chapel at Orvieto Cathedral.

works.name: The Annunciation (San Marco)
works.description: Fra Angelico's most famous work. Gabriel with multicolored wings approaches a sitting Virgin Mary in a portico/enclosed garden (hortus conclusus) with Corinthian columns and arches. In one version, Gabriel and Mary have arms crossed in identical positions. Among the first Western paintings to demonstrate linear perspective. Black lines mysteriously present between arcs of the loggia. The Cortona Annunciation features upside-down inscription 'Ecce Ancilla Domini' (Flemish technique), and Adam and Eve visible in left background.

works.name: San Marco Altarpiece
works.description: Altarpiece commissioned by Cosimo de' Medici, featuring infant Jesus holding a black orb above a small Crucifixion image. Saints Cosmas and Damian kneel on an Anatolian carpet (featuring Cancer and Pisces, symbolizing the Council of Florence). Often considered the first sacra conversazione. Includes Medici propaganda. Nine predella panels depict the story of Cosmas and Damian.

comprehensive_summary:
Fra Angelico (born Guido di Pietro) was an Italian Renaissance painter and Dominican friar, beatified in 1982, who combined Gothic and Renaissance styles. He is best known for decorating his own monastery of San Marco in Florence, where he painted frescoes in each cell of a complex rebuilt by Michelozzo di Bartolomeo under the patronage of Cosimo de' Medici. Savonarola's private chambers are preserved just yards from the most famous fresco.

His most famous work is the Annunciation at San Marco, in which the angel Gabriel with multicolored wings approaches a sitting Virgin Mary in a portico with Corinthian columns and arches (hortus conclusus style, with a garden shown to the left). In one version, Gabriel and Mary have their arms crossed in identical positions. The most famous version sits at the top of the entrance staircase and includes a daily prayer reminder. Gabriel is on the right and Mary on the left. These Annunciations were among the first Western paintings to demonstrate linear perspective. One version features golden rays emanating from God's hands and blue pendentives, and another has the word of God literally emerging from Gabriel's mouth. The less brightly-colored Cortona Annunciation uses the Flemish technique of an upside-down inscription 'Ecce Ancilla Domini,' shows Adam and Eve's expulsion in the left background, and has Latin words shooting from Gabriel's pink-clad, golden-winged form. Black lines are mysteriously present between arcs of the loggia.

The San Marco Altarpiece, commissioned by Cosimo de' Medici, features the infant Jesus holding a black orb above a small Crucifixion image, with Saints Cosmas and Damian kneeling on an Anatolian carpet featuring Cancer and Pisces (symbolizing the Council of Florence and Medici propaganda). It includes nine predella panels of Cosmas and Damian stories, and the Annalena Altarpiece is often considered the first sacra conversazione.

Other San Marco frescoes include a Crucifixion above portrait medallions of Dominican friars, a Mockery of Christ (disembodied hand brutalizing a blindfolded Jesus with a stick), and a Transfiguration (Moses and Elias under Jesus's outstretched arms, described as 'very, very yellow'). His St. Lawrence frescoes in the Niccoline Chapel show Lawrence in a flame-pattern cloak. He painted a Last Judgment with opened tombs at the bottom center, commissioned by the Camaldolese Order to commemorate Ambrogio Traversari, featuring a blue Jesus above an angel holding a cross and two blue-clad angels pointing toward a white walkway with a broken sarcophagus lid. He also painted a Deposition with landscape divided by three Gothic arches, a Coronation of the Virgin at the Uffizi, The Vision of Ezekiel (circular, with Creation and Gospel of John text), and a Prado Madonna with infant Christ feeling a pomegranate. His Adoration of the Magi (a tondo, collaboration with Fra Filippo Lippi) features a giant peacock at center. He began the fresco cycle in the San Brizio Chapel at Orvieto Cathedral; Luca Signorelli was hired to complete it and depicted himself alongside Fra Angelico in the finished frescoes. His student Benozzo Gozzoli also collaborated. His tomb is in the Basilica of Santa Maria sopra Minerva in Rome. David Hockney created an 'inside-out' six-sided version of his Annunciation, and Richard Hamilton digitally rendered it as a white gallery interior.
